_meta.authoringNotes: Prevalence figures from Mladina et al. Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2008 (PMID 18299023) cadaveric study of 2589 noses showing 89.2% asymmetry. AAO-HNS Clinical Practice Guideline: Nasal Obstruction (2020) and NICE CKS Septoplasty referenced for management. Septoplasty efficacy from Bugten et al. JAMA Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2016 (PMID 27348599) RCT of 203 patients. SCHIN study (Lancet 2019) referenced for septoplasty vs medical management.

h1Title: Deviated Nasal Septum: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: A deviated nasal septum is a displacement of the wall of cartilage and bone that divides the nasal cavity into two airways, causing one nostril to be narrower than the other. Cadaveric and CT studies show measurable septal asymmetry in 75-90% of adults, but only 10-25% experience symptoms severe enough to seek care. Typical presentation is chronic one-sided nasal blockage, mouth breathing, snoring, repeated sinus infections, or nosebleeds from the exposed deviated edge. Severe deviation contributes to obstructive sleep apnea, persistent rhinosinusitis, and reduced exercise tolerance. Septoplasty — an outpatient operation that straightens the septum without altering the external nose — resolves obstructive symptoms in roughly 75-85% of properly selected patients at 6-month follow-up.

keyStats.prevalence: Symptomatic deviation 12-18% of adults (US National Health Survey); measurable deviation on CT in 75-89% (Mladina et al. 2008)
keyStats.demographics: Men affected 2-3× more often than women among those needing surgery; congenital deviations seen in 5-10% of newborns after vaginal delivery
keyStats.avgAge: Most common presentation age 20-50; symptoms often delayed years after a childhood nasal injury
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 250,000 septoplasties performed yearly in the United States (Medicare/Marketscan claims)

definition: A deviated nasal septum (ICD-10: J34.2) is a structural deformity in which the partition between the right and left nasal cavities — composed of the quadrangular cartilage anteriorly and the perpendicular plate of the ethmoid and vomer posteriorly — lies off the midline. The deviation may be C-shaped, S-shaped, spurred, or dislocated from the maxillary crest, and may involve cartilage, bone, or both. The dorsal and caudal septum support the external nose, so anterior deviations distort the nasal tip and columella while posterior deviations compromise airflow without external change. Functionally, deviation narrows one nasal valve, alters airflow vectors, and disrupts the natural nasal cycle of alternating congestion, producing the sensation of one-sided obstruction. Contralateral compensatory turbinate hypertrophy frequently develops. Septal deviation is classified by site (anterior, dorsal, caudal, basal) and by impact (Mladina types 1-7, based on cadaveric morphology). It overlaps with internal nasal valve collapse, inferior turbinate hypertrophy, chronic rhinosinusitis, and nasal polyposis, all of which need to be assessed because septoplasty alone will not improve mixed obstruction. Otolaryngologists (ENT surgeons) and facial plastic surgeons manage the condition; severe combined deformity is treated by septorhinoplasty.

typesClassification.description: Deflection of the cartilaginous septum behind the nasal tip, producing visible asymmetry of the columella, airflow obstruction worst on lying on the unobstructed side, and difficulty breathing during exercise. Repair often requires extracorporeal or open techniques to reposition and suture the cartilage.

Smooth curvature of the dorsal septum into one cavity, frequently from old nasal trauma. Causes external nasal twist, asymmetric tip, and persistent unilateral obstruction. Standard septoplasty plus spreader graft placement restores midline alignment.

A localized bony or cartilaginous projection from the septum into one nasal cavity, often contacting the lateral wall and triggering contact-point headache (Sluder/sphenopalatine neuralgia). Endoscopic spur resection is curative in most cases.

The caudal septum slips off the bony groove of the maxillary crest, projecting into one airway and visible on rhinoscopy near the nasal floor. Repositioning and resuturing during septoplasty corrects the dislocation.

The septum curves in opposite directions in the anterior and posterior segments, narrowing both nasal cavities at different levels. Demands a thorough septoplasty with selective cartilage removal, scoring, and reinforcement.

primarySymptoms: Persistent one-sided nasal obstruction that does not resolve with decongestants and worsens during sleep on the affected side.
Alternating nasal blockage that swaps sides with the natural nasal cycle but stays predominantly on the deviated side.
Mouth breathing during sleep, dry mouth on waking, and snoring loud enough to disturb a bed partner.
Recurrent unilateral epistaxis from drying of the mucosa over the most prominent deviated edge, especially in heated or air-conditioned rooms.
Repeated sinus infections affecting the same side, with facial pain over the cheek or forehead, postnasal drip, and cough lasting more than 10 days.
Headache or referred facial pain from a septal spur contacting the lateral wall (contact-point headache), often relieved by topical lidocaine.
Loss of smell on the obstructed side, particularly when airflow cannot reach the olfactory cleft.
Whistling sound on inspiration or expiration from a perforation that may form over a deviated edge.
Reduced exercise tolerance with breathlessness disproportionate to fitness, especially during sustained aerobic effort.
Daytime sleepiness and morning headache when the deviation contributes to obstructive sleep apnea.
earlyWarningSigns: Children who consistently breathe through the mouth, snore loudly, or sleep with the head tilted toward the unobstructed side
Recurrent one-sided nosebleeds that respond to humidification but recur quickly
Repeated sinus infections always affecting the same side on imaging
Awareness that one nostril feels reliably more blocked than the other, even when otherwise well
Cosmetic asymmetry of the nasal tip or columella following a childhood facial injury
emergencySigns: Severe acute nasal obstruction with painful swelling of the septum after trauma — exclude septal hematoma, which can necrose cartilage within 72 hours if not drained
Visible bluish bulge of the septum on either side after a recent injury
Sudden complete unilateral nasal blockage with fever, facial pain, and proptosis suggesting orbital extension of acute sinusitis
Profuse uncontrolled epistaxis from the deviated edge that does not stop with 20 minutes of firm pressure
Sleep observation of witnessed apnoeas with gasping arousals — refer urgently for sleep study

causes.description: Compression of the fetal face during vaginal delivery, especially with cephalopelvic disproportion or forceps assistance, can dislocate the septum from the maxillary crest in the newborn. Studies of neonates show septal deviation in 5-22% of vaginal deliveries versus under 5% of caesarean births.

Sports impacts, falls, assaults, and motor vehicle injuries fracture or buckle the cartilaginous and bony septum. Even apparently minor injuries during childhood, when the septum is growing, may produce progressive deviation that becomes symptomatic only in adolescence or adulthood.

causes.cause: Asymmetric growth of the cartilaginous septum
causes.description: The quadrangular cartilage continues to grow until late adolescence within a fixed bony frame; mismatched growth rates produce buckling, ridges, and dislocations without any history of injury. This explains many deviations in patients with no recall of trauma.

Conditions such as Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos, and relapsing polychondritis weaken septal cartilage and predispose to deviation, septal perforation, and saddle-nose deformity. These should be considered when deviation is severe or accompanied by other connective tissue features.
Inadequate fixation of the caudal septum after rhinoplasty, septoplasty, or sinus surgery can leave or create a postoperative deviation. Revision septoplasty represents 5-10% of all septal surgery.

An untreated septal hematoma after trauma destroys the underlying cartilage by avascular necrosis. The resulting fibrotic scar contracts asymmetrically, producing a thickened, deviated, sometimes saddled septum.

riskFactors.description: Any nasal fracture or significant blow, even years before, raises the lifetime risk of symptomatic deviation roughly 3-5× compared with no trauma history. Contact-sport athletes have the highest incidence.
Men account for 65-75% of patients undergoing septoplasty in large US claims datasets, largely because of higher rates of facial trauma and contact-sport participation.
The cartilaginous septum grows fastest between ages 9 and 17. Trauma or asymmetric growth during this window produces deviations that persist into adult life and present in young adulthood.
Twin studies and pedigree analyses show modest familial clustering of nasal shape and septal morphology; relatives of a patient with marked deviation have roughly 2× background risk.
Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos, and Loeys-Dietz syndromes are associated with cartilaginous laxity and septal asymmetry; relapsing polychondritis can destroy and warp the septum.
Boxing, mixed martial arts, rugby, and ice hockey raise the lifetime risk of septal fracture roughly 4-6 fold. Protective face masks and gum-shield use reduce but do not eliminate risk.

riskFactors.factor: Allergic rhinitis and chronic mucosal swelling
riskFactors.description: Allergic mucosal swelling makes a pre-existing deviation symptomatic when it would otherwise be silent. Treating rhinitis often relieves symptoms enough to avoid surgery.

affectedDemographics: Men aged 20-50 account for the majority of septoplasty referrals across high-income countries.
Newborns delivered vaginally: septal deviation seen in 5-22% versus under 5% after caesarean section.
Boxers and combat-sport athletes: roughly 30-50% have measurable post-fight septal deviation in cross-sectional studies.
Patients with chronic rhinosinusitis: 40-60% have CT-documented septal deviation contributing to obstruction.
Children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy and obligate mouth breathing: 25-30% have coexistent significant septal deviation.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines a careful history, anterior rhinoscopy, and nasal endoscopy. The clinician asks which side is more blocked, whether the obstruction is constant or fluctuates, what makes it worse (lying down, exercise, allergy season), how nasal sprays affect it, and whether there has been previous trauma or surgery. Examination begins with anterior rhinoscopy using a nasal speculum to inspect the caudal septum, mucosa, turbinates, and any visible spur. Nasal endoscopy with a 2.7 mm or 4 mm rigid scope (sometimes flexible) inspects the middle meatus, posterior septum, choanae, and adenoid pad, ruling out polyps, masses, and adenoid hypertrophy. Decongestion with topical oxymetazoline differentiates fixed structural obstruction from reversible mucosal swelling. Cottle's manoeuvre and modified Cottle's manoeuvre assess internal nasal valve collapse. Patient-reported outcome scoring with the Nasal Obstruction Symptom Evaluation (NOSE) scale provides a baseline that the surgeon can compare with after surgery; scores above 30/100 generally indicate moderate to severe symptoms. CT of the paranasal sinuses is reserved for suspected rhinosinusitis, complex deformity, post-traumatic assessment, or pre-operative planning; routine CT for uncomplicated septal deviation is not recommended. Polysomnography is requested when sleep apnoea is suspected. Acoustic rhinometry and rhinomanometry quantify nasal resistance but are not required for diagnosis in routine practice.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies obvious anterior caudal deviation, spurs, septal perforations, mucosal disease, and turbinate hypertrophy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in the clinic in under 5 minutes. The doctor uses a small nasal speculum and a headlight or otoscope. No anaesthesia is needed; mild pressure may be felt.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Inspects the middle and posterior nasal cavity, middle meatus, choanae, and adenoids — areas a speculum cannot see
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A pediatric or 4 mm rigid scope is passed after topical xylometazoline and lidocaine spray. Takes 5-10 minutes. Mild tearing and a metallic taste are normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes fixed structural obstruction from reversible mucosal swelling
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sprays of oxymetazoline per nostril; the patient is re-examined after 10 minutes. Persistent obstruction confirms a structural cause.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the impact of obstruction on daily life and provides a baseline for measuring treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-item questionnaire scored 0-100. Takes 2 minutes to complete. Scores above 30 indicate significant obstruction.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of the paranasal sinuses
diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps septal anatomy in three planes, identifies sinus disease, plans complex revision surgery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-contrast scan, 5-10 minutes. Low-dose protocols deliver under 1 mSv. Reserved for surgical planning or coexisting sinus disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies internal nasal valve collapse contributing to obstruction
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner gently lateralizes the cheek skin while the patient breathes; improvement in airflow suggests valve collapse needing spreader grafts at surgery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies sleep apnoea severity when daytime sleepiness, witnessed apnoeas, or hypertension suggest OSA
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight study at a sleep laboratory or at home. Multiple sensors record breathing, oxygen, and brain activity. Apnoea-hypopnoea index above 15 confirms moderate-severe OSA.

treatmentOverview: Management is staged. Most patients begin with medical therapy aimed at the reversible mucosal component: intranasal corticosteroid sprays (fluticasone furoate 27.5 µg per spray, two sprays per nostril once daily) for 8-12 weeks, antihistamines if allergic rhinitis coexists, saline rinses, and a short course of oral or topical decongestants for symptomatic flares. AAO-HNS 2020 nasal-obstruction guidelines recommend at least 4-8 weeks of medical treatment before considering surgery in adults with no severe deformity. When medical therapy fails or the deviation produces marked structural obstruction (NOSE score ≥30 plus visible caudal deviation, septal spur, or radiologic confirmation), septoplasty is offered. Septoplasty is a 30-60 minute outpatient procedure under general anaesthesia; the surgeon raises mucoperichondrial flaps through a hemitransfixion or Killian incision, removes or scores deviated cartilage and bone, reinforces the remaining strut, and replaces the flaps with absorbable sutures. Internal nasal splints or silicone splints may be left in place for 5-7 days. Concurrent inferior turbinoplasty (radiofrequency, microdebrider, or submucosal resection) is added in roughly 60% of cases to address compensatory hypertrophy. Functional septorhinoplasty corrects combined external deformity and internal obstruction. In children, septoplasty is reserved for severe symptomatic deviation after age 6-8 because of concerns about midfacial growth. Postoperative care includes 1-2 weeks of saline rinses, avoidance of nose blowing for 2 weeks, no contact sports for 6 weeks, and ENT review at 2 and 6 weeks. Septal button or surgical closure is offered for symptomatic septal perforations. Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) is offered for coexistent moderate-severe sleep apnoea.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal corticosteroid spray (fluticasone furoate 55-110 µg/day or mometasone furoate 200 µg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces mucosal swelling, allergic inflammation, and compensatory turbinate hypertrophy. Used as first-line for at least 4-8 weeks before surgery is considered.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves NOSE score by 10-20 points in 40-60% of patients with mixed structural and mucosal obstruction

medicalTreatments.name: Saline nasal irrigation (240 mL isotonic per side, once or twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Removes crusts, allergens, and inflammatory mediators. Particularly helpful in dry climates and after exposure to dust, pollen, or pollutants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest symptomatic improvement in 30-50% of users; complements other therapies

medicalTreatments.name: Oral or topical antihistamines (loratadine, cetirizine, azelastine spray)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated when allergic rhinitis is contributing to mucosal swelling. Azelastine combined with intranasal corticosteroid (Dymista) is more effective than either alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces allergy-driven obstruction in 50-70% of patients with documented allergy

medicalTreatments.name: Short-course topical decongestant (oxymetazoline 0.05%, max 3-5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Provides rapid relief during acute viral or allergic flares and is used preoperatively to assess fixed obstruction. Long-term use causes rhinitis medicamentosa and should be avoided.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 80-90% short-term symptom relief; not a long-term option

medicalTreatments.name: Allergen-specific immunotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous or sublingual immunotherapy for patients with documented IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis aggravating obstruction. Course typically 3-5 years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sustained reduction in allergy symptoms in 60-80% with persistence after stopping

medicalTreatments.name: Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) for OSA
medicalTreatments.description: Mainstay for moderate-severe sleep apnoea coexisting with septal deviation. CPAP improves sleep, blood pressure, and daytime function but may be poorly tolerated if nasal obstruction is severe.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces apnoea-hypopnoea index by more than 90% when adherent; adherence improves after septoplasty in patients with high nasal resistance

surgicalOptions.name: Septoplasty (endoscopic or open)
surgicalOptions.description: Submucosal resection or repositioning of the deviated cartilage and bone through a hemitransfixion or Killian incision. Modern surgeons preserve as much cartilage as possible, scoring rather than removing, to maintain dorsal and caudal support.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 75-85% improvement in NOSE score at 6 months in randomized trials (Bugten et al. 2016, van Egmond et al. SCHIN trial 2019)

surgicalOptions.name: Septorhinoplasty (functional and aesthetic)
surgicalOptions.description: Combined correction of external nasal deformity and internal septal obstruction. Indicated for dorsal C-shaped or twist deformity, saddle nose, or post-traumatic asymmetry. Performed open or closed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient-reported breathing improvement in 80-90% and cosmetic satisfaction above 85% in modern series

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic septoplasty with concurrent turbinoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Combines endoscopic visualization for limited deviations or spurs with inferior turbinate reduction (radiofrequency, microdebrider, or submucous resection). Day-case procedure with reduced packing and faster recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom resolution in 85-90% at 12 months in published cohorts; turbinoplasty adds modest improvement in mixed obstruction

surgicalOptions.name: Extracorporeal septoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: The entire deviated septum is removed, reshaped on the back table, and replanted as a straight strut. Used for severe S-shaped or caudal deformity where in-situ correction would fail.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term straightening of severe deformity in 80-90% but technically demanding; revision rate 5-10%

surgicalOptions.name: Septal perforation repair
surgicalOptions.description: Performed for symptomatic perforations (whistling, crusting, epistaxis) using interposition grafts and bilateral mucosal flaps. A septal button can be offered to patients unfit for surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Closure success 70-90% in expert hands for perforations under 2 cm; lower for larger defects

alternativeTreatments.name: Internal nasal dilator strips (Breathe Right, Mute)
alternativeTreatments.description: External or internal devices that physically widen the nasal valve. Evidence is limited but they may help mild obstruction or during sport, particularly when internal valve collapse contributes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Positional sleep therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Sleeping on the side opposite the deviation, with the head elevated, may reduce snoring and obstruction. Useful for patients awaiting surgery or unfit for it.

whySeeSpecialist: An otolaryngologist or facial plastic surgeon can distinguish structural deviation from mucosal disease, identify coexisting turbinate hypertrophy, valve collapse, and sinus disease, and quantify obstruction with endoscopy and NOSE scoring. Specialist referral is essential when symptoms persist after a trial of intranasal corticosteroid, when there is a visible external deformity, or when sleep apnoea is suspected.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a surgeon who performs at least 50 septoplasties or septorhinoplasties a year, uses validated outcome measures (NOSE, SCHNOS), and has subspecialty training in rhinology or facial plastic surgery. Ask about cartilage-preserving techniques, revision rates, and concurrent management of valve and turbinate problems.
hospitalCriteria: Day-surgery unit equipped for endoscopic nasal procedures
Availability of rigid and flexible nasal endoscopes
Sinus CT capability for surgical planning
Multidisciplinary sleep medicine service for OSA assessment
Anaesthesia experience with shared airway procedures
keyFacilities: Facial plastic and reconstructive surgery clinics
Pediatric ENT services for childhood deviation
preventionStrategies: Wear a properly fitted mouthguard and protective face mask for contact and combat sports.
Use a seatbelt and airbag-equipped vehicles to reduce facial trauma during collisions.
Seek prompt evaluation of any nasal injury with visible swelling or bleeding to exclude septal hematoma.
Treat allergic rhinitis early with topical corticosteroids to reduce mucosal swelling that could mask or aggravate structural deviation.
Avoid prolonged use of decongestant sprays to prevent rhinitis medicamentosa, which compounds obstruction.
In children with persistent mouth breathing, snoring, or recurrent epistaxis seek an ENT review to identify and address contributing structural problems early.
lifestyleModifications: Use saline nasal irrigation once or twice daily, especially in heated or air-conditioned indoor environments.
Sleep on the side opposite the deviated airway with the head of the bed elevated 10-15 cm.
Maintain home humidity at 30-50% to reduce mucosal drying and crusting.
Avoid known allergens; use HEPA filtration in the bedroom if allergic rhinitis coexists.
Stop smoking — tobacco smoke worsens mucosal inflammation and post-surgical scarring.
Maintain a healthy weight; obesity worsens nasal congestion and sleep apnoea associated with deviation.
Limit alcohol within 3 hours of bedtime to reduce upper airway collapse and snoring.

recommended: Adequate hydration (2-2.5 L per day) to keep nasal secretions thin
Anti-inflammatory foods (oily fish, leafy greens, berries) for patients with allergic rhinitis
Vitamin C and zinc from food sources to support mucosal immunity
Warm fluids (broth, herbal tea) during congestion flares
avoid: Alcohol within 3 hours of sleep, which worsens nasal congestion
Dairy products in patients who notice increased mucus thickness after consumption
Excessive salt, which can aggravate fluid retention and turbinate swelling
Hot or spicy foods immediately before sleep in patients with vasomotor rhinitis

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise is encouraged; mouth breathing during high-intensity work is normal and not harmful. Avoid contact sports for 6 weeks after septoplasty and consider a protective face mask if returning to combat or impact sports. Internal nasal dilator strips may help symptomatic obstruction during running and cycling.
dailyManagement: Use a once-daily intranasal corticosteroid spray in the morning, aimed away from the septum to prevent dryness.
Perform saline irrigation 5-10 minutes after the corticosteroid spray, not before.
Track NOSE score monthly while on medical therapy to detect improvement or worsening.
Recognize and treat flares of allergic rhinitis early with non-sedating antihistamines.
Keep a humidifier in the bedroom during winter or in dry climates.
Avoid more than 5 days of topical decongestant in a row to prevent rebound congestion.
prognosis: Outcome is excellent when patient selection and surgical technique are sound. Randomized trials (Bugten et al. 2016, van Egmond et al. SCHIN 2019) show that septoplasty produces a mean 25-30 point improvement in NOSE score at 6 and 12 months, with 75-85% of patients reporting symptom resolution and durable benefit at 5 years. Failure is most often due to unrecognized internal nasal valve collapse, untreated allergic rhinitis, or persistent compensatory turbinate hypertrophy, all of which are now routinely assessed preoperatively. Revision septoplasty rates are 5-10% in tertiary series. Untreated symptomatic deviation does not reverse and may progress as adjacent structures remodel; chronic mouth breathing in children alters facial growth, and severe obstruction contributes to sleep apnoea, hypertension, and reduced quality of life. Septal perforation is the most common surgical complication (0.5-3%), followed by saddle-nose deformity, septal hematoma, and persistent obstruction. Mortality is extremely rare.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients return to desk work within 7 days and to non-contact exercise at 2 weeks. Internal splints are removed at 5-7 days. Nasal breathing improves over 4-8 weeks as swelling resolves; final result at 3-6 months. Contact sport, heavy lifting, and high-altitude travel should be avoided for 6 weeks.
complications: Septal hematoma after trauma or surgery — bluish swelling of the septum within 72 hours, requires urgent drainage to prevent cartilage necrosis and saddle nose.
Septal perforation (0.5-3% after septoplasty) producing whistling, crusting, and unilateral epistaxis — symptomatic perforations need surgical closure or button.
Saddle-nose deformity from over-resection of dorsal cartilage or untreated hematoma — requires reconstructive grafting.
Persistent obstruction from unaddressed turbinate hypertrophy or valve collapse — diagnosed by post-operative endoscopy and treated with secondary procedures.
Olfactory disturbance (transient in 5-15%, permanent under 1%) — usually resolves with mucosal healing.
Worsening of obstructive sleep apnoea or new-onset apnoea if untreated severe deviation contributes to airway collapse.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery (AAO-HNS) — Nasal Obstruction guideline
supportResources.description: Clinical practice guideline summarizing diagnosis and management of nasal obstruction including septal deviation.

supportResources.name: NHS — Septoplasty
supportResources.description: Plain-language patient guide to septoplasty in the UK National Health Service.

supportResources.name: ENT UK — Patient information leaflets
supportResources.description: Patient leaflets on deviated septum, septoplasty, and recovery.

supportResources.name: American Rhinologic Society — Patient education
supportResources.description: Patient-facing explanations of nasal anatomy, septal deviation, and treatment options.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause nasal obstruction and pressure, but chronic sinusitis features mucopurulent discharge, facial pain, hyposmia, and mucosal disease on CT lasting more than 12 weeks. Septal deviation alone shows no mucosal opacification; the two often coexist and CT helps distinguish their relative contributions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis produces bilateral congestion, sneezing, itching, and clear watery rhinorrhoea triggered by allergens, and reverses with antihistamines and intranasal corticosteroid. Septal deviation produces predominantly one-sided fixed obstruction unresponsive to medical therapy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nasal Polyps
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polyps are pale, soft, painless mucosal swellings visible on rhinoscopy or endoscopy and bilateral in most cases; they shrink with intranasal or oral corticosteroid. Septal deviation is a firm, fixed midline cartilaginous deformity that does not shrink medically.

confusedWithConditions.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OSA is a sleep-related collapse of the pharyngeal airway; daytime sleepiness, witnessed apnoeas, and apnoea-hypopnoea index above 5 on polysomnography define it. Septal deviation can contribute to OSA but is not the primary site of collapse; both are often present and require separate management.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Often coexists with septal deviation; mechanical obstruction impairs sinus ventilation and drainage.

relatedConditions.name: Recurrent Nosebleeds
relatedConditions.relevance: Drying of the mucosa over the most prominent deviated edge is a common cause of recurrent unilateral epistaxis.

relatedConditions.name: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe deviation raises nasal resistance and contributes to upper-airway collapse during sleep.

relatedConditions.name: Nasal Polyps
relatedConditions.relevance: Polyps and septal deviation frequently occur together in chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Allergic mucosal swelling makes a previously silent deviation symptomatic and must be treated alongside any structural correction.

faqs.question: What is a deviated nasal septum?
faqs.answer: A deviated nasal septum is a sideways shift of the cartilage and bone wall that separates the right and left nasal cavities. The deviation makes one airway narrower than the other and can cause one-sided blockage, mouth breathing, snoring, recurrent nosebleeds, or repeated sinus infections.

faqs.question: How common is a deviated septum?
faqs.answer: Measurable deviation appears in roughly 75-90% of adults on CT or cadaveric studies, but only 12-18% have symptoms severe enough to seek treatment. Men are affected 2-3 times more often than women among those who need surgery.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for a deviated septum?
faqs.answer: Surgery is reserved for patients whose symptoms do not improve after 4-8 weeks of intranasal corticosteroid and saline irrigation, or who have visible external deformity, recurrent epistaxis, or sinus disease driven by the deviation. Most patients with mild deviation manage well without surgery.

faqs.question: What is septoplasty?
faqs.answer: Septoplasty is a 30-60 minute outpatient operation under general anaesthesia in which the surgeon repositions or removes the deviated parts of the cartilage and bone through a small incision inside the nose. The external shape of the nose is not changed. Most patients return to work within a week.

faqs.question: Does septoplasty change the appearance of the nose?
faqs.answer: Standard septoplasty does not change the external shape of the nose because the dorsal and caudal cartilage that support the nasal pyramid are preserved. If both breathing and shape need to be corrected, a combined functional septorhinoplasty is performed.

faqs.question: How long does septoplasty recovery take?
faqs.answer: Splints are removed at 5-7 days and most people return to desk work within a week. Light exercise resumes at 2 weeks. Final airflow improvement takes 4-8 weeks as swelling settles, and contact sport should be avoided for 6 weeks. Saline rinses help during recovery.

faqs.question: How successful is septoplasty?
faqs.answer: Randomized trials show 75-85% of properly selected patients report significant or complete relief of nasal obstruction at 6-12 months after septoplasty, with a 25-30 point improvement on the NOSE quality-of-life score that is sustained at 5 years.

faqs.question: Can a deviated septum cause sleep apnoea?
faqs.answer: A severe deviation increases nasal resistance and contributes to obstructive sleep apnoea but is rarely the only cause. Septoplasty alone usually does not cure moderate-severe OSA but can improve CPAP tolerance and reduce snoring when combined with sleep-medicine management.

faqs.question: Will a deviated septum get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Deviations that are stable in adulthood usually do not progress, but compensatory turbinate hypertrophy, allergic rhinitis, and ageing mucosa can make a stable deviation feel worse over years. New trauma or surgery can also alter septal alignment.

faqs.question: Can I treat a deviated septum without surgery?
faqs.answer: Mild and moderate symptoms often respond to intranasal corticosteroid sprays, saline irrigation, treatment of allergic rhinitis, and nasal dilator strips. These do not straighten the septum but reduce mucosal swelling and improve airflow enough to avoid surgery in many patients.

faqs.question: Is septoplasty painful?
faqs.answer: Modern septoplasty is performed under general anaesthesia and most patients report mild to moderate discomfort, pressure, and congestion rather than sharp pain. Simple analgesics (paracetamol with or without ibuprofen) control symptoms in over 90% of cases.

faqs.question: What is a septal hematoma?
faqs.answer: A septal hematoma is a collection of blood under the lining of the septum after trauma, appearing as a bluish bulge on one or both sides. It must be drained within 72 hours to prevent cartilage necrosis and a permanent saddle-nose deformity, which is why any nasal injury with swelling needs prompt review.

faqs.question: Can children have septoplasty?
faqs.answer: Septoplasty in children is reserved for severe symptomatic deviation after age 6-8 because of concerns about midfacial growth. Limited cartilage-sparing techniques are preferred, and surgery is often delayed until adolescence unless obstruction is severe or post-traumatic.

faqs.question: Will my insurance cover septoplasty?
faqs.answer: Most insurers cover septoplasty when documented symptomatic nasal obstruction has failed at least 4-8 weeks of medical therapy, with structural deviation confirmed on examination or CT. Purely cosmetic rhinoplasty is not covered. Pre-authorization is usually required.

faqs.question: How is a deviated septum diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, based on history, anterior rhinoscopy with a nasal speculum, and nasal endoscopy. The NOSE quality-of-life questionnaire scores severity. CT of the sinuses is added only when sinus disease, complex deformity, or revision surgery is suspected.

faqs.question: Can a deviated septum cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Yes, when a septal spur contacts the lateral nasal wall it can trigger contact-point headaches and referred facial pain. Local anaesthetic applied to the contact site typically abolishes the pain within minutes; endoscopic spur removal is curative for selected patients.

faqs.question: Does a deviated septum cause snoring?
faqs.answer: A severe deviation that raises nasal resistance often contributes to snoring by promoting mouth breathing during sleep. Septoplasty reduces snoring loudness in 50-70% of patients but rarely eliminates it when oropharyngeal collapse coexists.

faqs.question: Can a deviated septum affect smell?
faqs.answer: Severe deviations can block airflow to the olfactory cleft and reduce smell on the affected side. Smell usually improves after septoplasty, especially when allergic rhinitis and nasal polyps are also treated, but pre-existing chronic anosmia from olfactory nerve damage does not reverse.

faqs.question: Are there risks with septoplasty?
faqs.answer: Septoplasty is safe in experienced hands but carries a small risk of septal perforation (0.5-3%), bleeding, infection, persistent obstruction, transient olfactory disturbance, and saddle-nose deformity from over-resection. Revision rates are 5-10% in published series.

faqs.question: What happens if a deviated septum is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated symptomatic deviation does not reverse and can lead to chronic mouth breathing, recurrent sinusitis, snoring, worsening sleep apnoea, and reduced quality of life. In children, persistent obstruction may alter facial growth. Asymptomatic deviation needs no treatment.

faqs.question: How do I know if my nasal blockage is from a deviated septum or allergies?
faqs.answer: Allergic blockage is usually bilateral, fluctuates with seasons, comes with sneezing and itching, and improves with antihistamines or steroid spray. Septal deviation produces predominantly one-sided fixed blockage that persists after decongestion. An ENT examination distinguishes them definitively.
